title: 第 6 節
en: performing human acts, and as if possessed of a human body. This is explained as metaphorical (rupaka) language. If a river is addressed as driving on a chariot, it would be impossible to take this literally, and hence it is to be taken as a rupakapravada. The prevalent opinion, however, seems to have been that the gods had to be conceived as endowed with a human form, though, from the old Veclanta point of view, they were various manifestations only of the Atman or the Supreme Self The classification of the Vedic deities in three classes according to the localities in which they are supposed chiefly to dwell, though very imperfect in its details, deserves nevertheless to be carefully examined as a first attempt at theological speculation.

We shall see that for a proper understanding of the Vedic gods and their relation to each other, this view of their activity, nay of their very essence, as determined by the sphere in which they act, is extremely important and useful. At first sight the idea that there were originally three gods only, Agni, Indra, and Aditya, representing certain phenomena on earth, in the air, and in the sky, and that these received different names according to the special work assigned to them, seems very artificial, and therefore wrong. And yet there is some truth in it, if we do not take it in too literal a sense. We must not suppose that Agni, after having been named and recognised as a special deity, was afterwards changed into Dravinodas, Tanunapat, TvashtfW or Surya, or that Indra was named at a later time Vayu, Rudra, Par(/anya, or that Aditya assumed the form of Ushas, the Asvinau, of Vishnu or Vl] THE THIRTY-THREE GODS. 479 Varuna. Yet there is truth hidden in Yaska's theory, namely this, that though Agni may have started from the fire on the hearth, he was not restricted to it, but was recognised in all the manifestations of light on earth and in the sky. Indra, again, though originally the giver of rain, could be recognised not only as the conqueror of the clouds, but as the agent in all that takes place in the air, while Aditya was accepted not simply as the sun, but as the active power in the whole sky. The fault of Yaska's threefold division is not that it is too general, but that in some cases it is really too narrow, because there are Devatas who extend their influence over more than one of these three spheres of nature. Agni, for instance, though originally recognised in the house-fire, and therefore belonging to the earth, is likewise seen by the Vedic poets as present in the lightning of the air, in the brightness of the sky, nay, according to a very common conception, in the waters also, whether the clouds or the sea, into which he plunges every evening, and from which he rises in the morning. In some cases the names given to the divine agents in the three realms of nature vary according to the special work performed by each of them, in others the general names of Agni, Indra, Aditya are retained throughout. In these cases Agni, Indra, and Aditya often overshadow the special gods of their own spheres, nay they encroach even on the spheres not specially their own, and thus produce a confusion which is often very perplexing to those who want to find in the Veda their own views, or those which they have derived from the more specialised mythology of other nations.

Following, therefore, the guidance of Yaska, we begin with Agni, whether he belongs to the earth or the earth belongs to him. To him, according to Yaska, belong also the Morning libation (prata/isavanam), the spring among the seasons, the Gayatrl among the metres, the TrivWt among the stomas (praises), the Rathantara among the Samans (songs). Much of this is of course secondary and artificial, but it rests on a true principle. Agni is supposed to have associates and followers (Devaganas), and these are not only gods and goddesses, but likewise a number of objects with which he is thought to be more or less intimately connected. Thus the deities invoked in the Apri hymns x, are all supposed to be his, or he is supposed to be connected with every one of them.

First of all, two names of Agni, the god of fire, are given as synonymous, viz. 2. (ratavedas (knowing all things 2), and 3. Vaisvanara (belonging to all men). Then follow the names of the Apri deities 3 : —
5. Idhma(fuel). 8. I7a, also called llitah 6. Tanunapat (self-born). (implored).
1 Vedic Hymns, S. B.E., vol. xlvi, p. 10.

- With reference to Kv. VI, 15, 13, visva veda #anima #ataveda/i, I translate omniscient, not all-possessor. That vedas does not occur by itself, is no objection, see compounds with (/oshas, oshas, &c. See, however, Vedic Hymns, vol. xlvi.
'' See History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 413 seq. Modern as these Apri hymns seem to us, afri hymns are known in the Avesta.
9. Barhis (the turf-altar). 10. Dvara/i (the doors of heaven, the East). n. Ushasanakta(dawnand night).
13. Tisro Devih (the three goddesses, Agnayi, Pnthivi, Ha,, or Bharati, Ilk, Sarasvati)1.
Tvash^r i (the carpenter, creator). Vanaspati (the sacrificial tree). 16. Svahakntis (the invocations).

Many of these names refer to various objects invoked by the poets in the so-called Apri hymns. Agni, if not exactly identified with them, was supposed to be represented by them, and to take cognisance of the invocations addressed to their names. Then follows a long list of other names which are likewise considered as names of objects sacred to Agni, as we should say, and often mentioned in hymns addressed to this deity. Such are :- II. I.
zh: 執行人類的行為，彷彿具有人類的身體。這被解釋為隱喻（rupaka）語言。如果河流被描述為駕駛戰車，則不可能字面理解，因此應將其視為隱喻論述（rupakapravada）。然而，當時普遍的看法似乎是，提婆必須被理解為賦予人類形體，儘管從古老的吠檀多觀點來看，它們不過是梵我（Atman）或最高自我（Supreme Self）的各種顯現。根據提婆主要居住的地點將吠陀諸神分為三類，儘管其細節上相當不完善，仍值得仔細審視，作為神學思辨的最初嘗試。

我們將看到，為了適當理解吠陀諸神及其相互間的關係，這種關於它們的活動、乃至它們本質的觀點——由它們所作用的領域決定——是極其重要且有用的。乍看之下，原本只有三位神祇——阿格尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）和阿迪提亞（Aditya）的想法，它們分別代表地上、空中和天上的某些現象，而這三位神祇因被賦予的特殊職能而獲得不同的名號，這似乎非常人為，因此是錯誤的。然而，如果我們不把它理解得過於字面，其中確實存有某些真理。我們不應該想像，阿格尼在被命名並被認定為一位特殊的提婆後，隨後便改變成德拉維諾達斯（Dravinodas）、塔努納帕特（Tanunapat）、特瓦什特里（Tvashtr）或蘇利耶（Surya），也不應該想像因陀羅後來被命名為伐由（Vayu）、魯陀羅（Rudra）、帕爾尼亞（Paranya），或者阿迪提亞取得了烏莎斯（Ushas）、雙馬童（the Asvinau）、毘濕奴（Vishnu）或伐樓那（Varuna）的形態。然而，耶斯卡（Yaska）的理論中隱含著真理，即：雖然阿格尼可能源自家中的火焰，但它並不局限於此，而是在地上和天上光的一切顯現中被認識。同樣地，因陀羅雖然本來是降雨的給予者，但它不僅能被認識為雲的征服者，也能被認識為發生在空中一切事物的推動者；而阿迪提亞則不只被接受為太陽，更被接受為整個天空的主動力量。耶斯卡三分法的缺陷不在於它過於寬泛，而在於在某些情況下它確實過於狹隘，因為有些提婆的影響力延伸到這三個自然領域中的不止一個。例如，阿格尼雖然本來在家中的火焰中被認識，因此屬於地球領域，但吠陀詩人也同樣在空中的閃電、天空的光亮中看到它，甚至根據一種很常見的觀念，在水中也看到它——無論是雲或海洋，它在每個傍晚潛入其中，在清晨又從中升起。在有些情況下，賦予三個自然領域中的神聖推動者的名號因各自所執行的特殊職能而有所不同；在其他情況下，阿格尼、因陀羅、阿迪提亞的通常名號則始終被保留。在這些情況下，阿格尼、因陀羅和阿迪提亞往往掩蓋了各自領域內的特殊提婆，甚至侵越並非特別屬於它們的領域，因而造成了一種混亂，這種混亂對於那些想在吠陀中尋找自己觀點、或從其他民族的更為專門化的神話中汲取觀點的人來說，往往是令人困惑的。
480 阿格尼。[第　章
I. 阿耆尼

因此，遵循耶斯卡（Yaska）的指導，我們從阿格尼（Agni）開始，無論他屬於大地，抑或大地屬於他。根據耶斯卡所言，晨間奠祭（prata/isavanam）、四時中的春季、韻律中的迦雅特里（Gayatrl）、讚歌中的特里維特（TrivWt）以及讚美歌中的拉坦塔拉（Rathantara），皆隸屬於他。這些聯繫當然多為次生的與人為的，但卻奠基於一個真確的原則。阿格尼被認為擁有眾多伴侶與追隨者（Devaganas），而這些不僅是神祇與女神，亦包括與他思想上或多或少密切相關的諸多對象。因此，阿普里讚歌（Apri hymns）中所祈禱的諸位神祇，皆被認為屬於他，或者說他被認為與他們每一位都有所關聯。
首先，火神阿耆尼（Agni）的兩個名號被列為同義詞，即 2. 羅陀吠陀斯（ratavedas，通曉萬物者），及 3. 毘濕瓦那羅（Vaisvanara，屬於眾人者）。隨後是阿普里提婆神（Apri deities）的名號：—
I. 4. 德剌毗沃達斯（財富施予者）；7. 那羅薩姆沙（人類讚頌者）。
5. Idhma（燃料）。8. I7a，亦稱 Illtah（祈求者）。6. Tanunapat（自生者）。
梵文頌讚，《東方聖典叢書》（S.B.E.）第四十六卷，第 10 頁。
關於《梨俱吠陀》VI, 15, 13，visva veda #anima #ataveda/i，我譯為「全知者」（omniscient），而非「全有者」（all-possessor）。吠陀（veda）一詞並不單獨出現，這並非異議，見其與 oshas、oshas 等詞的複合詞。然參見《吠陀讚歌》第四十六卷。
'■' 參見《古代梵文文獻史》，第 413 頁及以下。儘管《阿普里讚歌》（Apri hymns）對我們而言似乎相當現代，類似的讚歌在《阿維斯陀》（Avesta）中也為人所知。
9. 巴爾希斯（Barhis，草祭壇）。10. 德瓦拉（Dvara/i，天界之門、東方）。11. 烏沙薩納克塔（Ushasanakta，晨曦與夜晚）。
12. 提婆賀塔盧（Daivya Hotarau）（兩位提婆祭司）。
13. 三位提婆女神（Tisro Devih）——即阿格那依（Agnayi）、普里塔維（Prthivi）、哈（Ha）或巴拉蒂（Bharati）、伊拉（Ilk）、薩拉斯瓦蒂（Sarasvati）1。
特瓦什特里（木匠、造物主）。婆那斯帕提（祭祀樹）。16. 色婆訶祈禱文（祈禱詞）。

許多這些名字指涉詩人在所謂的《阿普里讚歌》(Apri hymns) 中所祈請的各種對象。阿格尼 (Agni)，若非確切地與它們同一，便被設想為由它們所代表，並對指向它們名字的祈禱予以關注。隨後是一長串其他名字，同樣被視為聖於阿格尼的對象之名，如我們所說的那樣，並在指向此神祇的讚歌中時常被提及。這些包括：II. I.
阿史瓦（馬）
伊舒（箭）。
薩庫尼（鳥）。
阿斯瓦尼（Asva^ani，馬鞭）。
莫屯卡（青蛙）。
烏盧卡拉（臼）。
阿剎沙（骰子）。
